definition: A conductive solid or mesh enclosure used to block electromagnetic fields. Also known as a Faraday shield. Named after its inventor Michael Faraday.
The sound field distant enough from the sound source that the SPL decreases by 6dB for each doubling of the distance from the source.
The viewer positioned at the farthest distance from the screen as defined by the viewing area.
1. In audio, unwanted noise caused by the loop of an audio system’s output back to its input. 2. In a control system, data supplied to give an indication of system performance or status.
The extra margin added (usually 6dB) into the needed acoustic gain (NAG) formula that represents extra gain that a sound system may have available before the onset of feedback.
A technology that uses total internal reflection of light along a transparent fiber to transmit information.
In interlaced video, one-half of a video frame containing every other line of information. Each interlaced video frame contains two fields.
A device or process that blocks or passes certain elements of a signal.
Any technology, hardware, or software that regulates the traffic permitted to enter or exit a network. Firewalls control access across network boundaries.

Any firewall or Application-layer gateway (ALG) is expected to provide mechanisms that allows traffic to traverse through the firewall/ALG to reach an intended destination. Firewall traversal is provided in multiple ways, including NAT traversal, IPSec tunnels, IP ACLs (access control lists), or port-based ACLs.
A type of software that is stored in nonvolatile memory in a piece of hardware.
A luminaire that is mounted or fixed in place.
The number of times a cable can be bent before it breaks. A wire with more strands or twists per meter will have a greater flex life than one with a lower number of strands or fewer twists per meter.
The distance between the center of a lens and the point where the image comes into focus. The shorter the focal length, the wider the angle of the image will be.
The U.S. customary unit of illuminance. The incident light measured when 1 lumen of light is spread over an area of 1 square foot. Its symbol is fc. 1 foot-candle = 10.76 lux.
The footlambert is the U.S. customary unit for luminance. It is equal to 1/π candela per square foot. Its symbol is fl. 1 footlambert = 3.43 candela per square meter.
The number of frames per second sent from a source.
The number of complete cycles in a specified period of time. It is measured in hertz (Hz). 1Hz = 1 cycle per second.
A signal viewed as frequency versus amplitude is in the frequency domain. This allows you to view the amount of energy present at different frequencies.
The amplitude response versus frequency for a given device.

A flat lens in which the curvature of a normal lens surface has been collapsed in such a way that concentric circles are impressed on the lens surface. A Fresnel lens is often used for the condenser lens in overhead projectors, in rear-screen projection systems, and in Fresnel spotlights.
An image projection system where the image is projected from a source on the viewer’s side of the screen.
A form of bi-directional data transmission in which messages may simultaneously travel in both directions.
A high-definition video mode with a resolution of 1920×1080 pixels.
Known as pure tone, the lowest frequency in a harmonic series.
